1: lĕmāšîaḥô lĕkôreš — to his anointed, to Cyrus. The term māšîaḥ (מָשִׁיחַ, anointed) is applied to a foreign king — a uniquely bold claim. YHWH grasps Cyrus's right hand (ḥāzaqtî bîmînô), the posture of divine empowerment. The verse opens with the oracular messenger formula angled toward a pagan ruler, asserting that YHWH is the hidden sovereign behind Persian conquest.
2: wāhădûrîm 'ăyaššēr — and the rugged places I will make level. The Pi'el of יָשַׁר (yeyyōšēr) denotes active straightening. Doors of bronze (daltôt nĕḥušet) and bars of iron (bĕrîḥê barzèl) are broken — Herodotus records Cyrus entering Babylon via the Euphrates channel under bronze gates. The verse uses *yiqtol* verbs throughout, framing YHWH's actions as imminent and certain.
3: maṭmônê ḥōšek — treasures of darkness and hidden riches of secret places. These are material spoils granted so Cyrus may know (tēda') the personal name YHWH. The clause qōrĕ'akā bišmekā — who calls you by your name — signals intimate foreknowledge; Cyrus is named prophetically before his birth.
4: lĕma'an 'abdî ya'ăqōb — for the sake of my servant Jacob. The cosmic military campaign of Cyrus has a theological purpose: Israel's redemption. The phrase wĕ'epĕsinnĕkā — though you have not known me — makes divine sovereignty explicit: YHWH acts through unknowing instruments. The verb ekannĕkā (Pi'el of כָּנָה) — I surname/title you — indicates honorific designation.
5: 'ănî YHWH wĕ'ên 'ôd — I am YHWH and there is none else. The foundational monotheistic formula ('ên 'ôd) recurs through Isaiah 45:5–6, 14, 18, 21–22. Even where Cyrus does not acknowledge YHWH, YHWH girds him — the verb wa'a'azzĕrĕkā (Pi'el of אָזַר, to gird/equip) — underscoring sovereignty over those outside covenant.
6: lĕma'an yēdĕ'û — so that they may know. The universal scope (mimmizraḥ šemeš wûmimma'ărabô, from east to west) is stated: all nations are to recognize YHWH's uniqueness. The infinitive of purpose drives the oracle — revelation of divine identity is the telos of historical events.
7: yôṣēr 'ôr ûbôrē' ḥōšek — forming light and creating darkness. Two Qal participles (yôṣēr from יָצַר; bôrē' from בָּרָא) assert continuous creative activity. The climax is the theologically sharp bôrē' rā' — creating evil/disaster. This is not moral evil but calamity (rā' as adversity) — asserting YHWH's sovereignty over all reality, countering Persian dualism (Ahura Mazda vs. Angra Mainyu).
8: har'îpû šāmayim — drip/rain down, O heavens. The verb rā'ap̄ (Hiphil) means to let fall in drops; heavens and earth are called to release righteousness (ṣedeq) and salvation (yešû'āh). The imagery is agricultural (rain bringing forth crops) applied to cosmic justice: salvation sprouts from the earth (tiṣmaḥ).
9: hôy rāb̠ 'et-yōṣrô — woe to one who strives with his Maker. The hôy oracle (woe cry) opens a lawsuit metaphor. ḥèreś (potsherd) among the potsherds of the ground — the creature is earthenware arguing with the potter. The rhetorical questions (hăyō'mar ḥōmer lĕyōṣĕrô) make the absurdity concrete. The Qal imperfect yārib̠ — to contend, bring legal suit — frames the creature's complaint as legal overreach.
10: hôy 'ōmēr lĕ'āb̠ — woe to one who says to a father, 'What are you begetting?'. The paired woe oracle extends the analogy from potter/clay to parent/child. Both challenge the prerogative of the creator/parent. The point is that creatures cannot interrogate the purposes of their origin.
11: šā'ălûnî — ask me of things to come. The form is a Qal imperative plural with suffix — YHWH invites interrogation of his purposes regarding his children and the works of his hands (pō'al yādāy). This is not sarcasm but genuine openness: YHWH will explain — through his word to prophets — what he is doing.
12: 'ănōkî 'āśîtî 'èreṣ — I made the earth and created man upon it. Creation credentials undergird the political claims of Isaiah 45:1–7. The series of *qatal* perfects ('āśîtî, bārā'tî, nāṭîtî, ṣiwwêtî) — made, created, stretched, commanded — establishes unassailable authority to commission Cyrus.
13: hā'îrtî bĕ-ṣedeq — I have stirred him up in righteousness. The verb 'ûr (Hiphil, hā'îrtî) means to arouse/awaken — Cyrus is YHWH's awakened instrument. The purpose is double: to build the city (yibĕneh 'îrî) and release the exiles (gālûtî yĕšallēaḥ). The clause lō' bĕmḥîr wĕlō' bĕšōḥad — not for price, not for bribe — emphasizes sheer sovereign grace.
14: yĕgî'a mĭṣrayim — the wealth of Egypt. Three nations — Egypt, Cush, Sabeans — will transfer their wealth and come in chains (bazzîqqîm) and bow, confessing 'ak bĕkā 'ēl wĕ'ên 'ôd — Surely God is in you, and there is no other. The chains here are voluntary submission, not conquest. This is the reversal of exodus: former oppressor nations becoming worshippers.
15: 'ēl mistattēr — a God who hides himself. The Hithpa'el participle of סָתַר — who conceals himself — describes YHWH's mysterious self-veiling in history, even as he is confessed as 'ĕlōhê yiśrā'ēl môšîa' — God of Israel, Savior. The tension between hiddenness and saving action is a motif throughout Isaiah.
16: bōšû wĕgam-niklĕmû — they are put to shame and also confounded. The idol-makers (ḥārāšê ṣîrîm, craftsmen of idols) face corporate humiliation. The Pi'el yēlĕkû — they go off together — in shame. The contrast with Isaiah 45:17 (Israel saved with eternal salvation) is rhetorically sharp.
17: niôša' yiśrā'ēl — Israel is saved by YHWH with an everlasting salvation. The Niphal of יָשַׁע — passive, Israel as recipient of salvation. The modifier tĕšû'at 'ôlāmîm — everlasting salvation — is unique in Isaiah; temporal qualifiers emphasize the finality and permanence of what YHWH accomplishes.
18: lō'-tōhû bĕrā'āh — not in chaos did he form it. YHWH created earth to be inhabited (lāšèbet yĕṣārāh) — yāšab̠ (to dwell) is the telos of creation. The use of tōhû (formless void, cf. Genesis 1:2) asserts that chaos was not the intended state. Monotheistic purpose underlies all creation. The repetition of 'ănî YHWH wĕ'ên 'ôd anchors the theology.
19: lō'-bassēter dibbartî — I have not spoken in secret, in a land of darkness. YHWH's word is public, given in the open and declared in advance — contrasting with the oracular obscurities of pagan divination. The verb 'āmartî — I said — and dibbartî — I spoke — are *qatal* perfects of completed, dependable revelation.
20: hiqqābĕṣû ûbō'û — assemble yourselves and come. The call is universal: survivors of the nations (pĕlîṭê haggôyim). The irony — those who pray to a wooden god (wĕmitpallĕlîm 'el-'ēl lō' yôšîa') have no knowledge — sets up the contrast with YHWH who saves. The verb yôšîa' (Hiphil of ישׁע) — cannot save — deliberately echoes môšîa' of Isaiah 45:15.
21: haggîdû wĕhaggîšû — declare and present your case. Legal language: YHWH challenges the nations to produce counsel (yā'ṣû) that predicted events. The rhetorical question ûmî hiš-mî'a — who made this heard? — anticipates the answer: YHWH alone. The divine title 'ēl ṣaddîq ûmôšîa' — righteous God and Savior — combines covenantal justice with redemptive action.
22: pĕnû 'ēlay wĕhiwwāšĕ'û — Turn to me and be saved, all the ends of the earth!. The Qal imperative pĕnû (turn) and Niphal imperative hiwwāšĕ'û (be saved) form a paired command: turning and salvation are simultaneous. The universal scope (kol-'apĕsê 'āreṣ) is maximally inclusive. The theological ground: 'ănî 'ēl wĕ'ên 'ôd.
23: bî niš-ba'tî — By myself I have sworn. Divine oath formula (cf. Genesis 22:16). The word that has gone from YHWH's mouth (yāṣā' mippî ṣĕdāqāh) will not return empty. The prediction: kol bèrek tikra' — every knee shall bow — and kol lāšôn tiššābaʿ — every tongue shall swear. The *yiqtol* verbs convey inevitable future reality. NT: Philippians 2:10–11; Romans 14:11 cite this verse for Christ's universal lordship.
24: 'ak bYHWH lî 'āmar — Only in YHWH are righteousness and strength. The particle 'ak (only, surely) introduces the exclusive claim. The phrase 'ēlāyw yāb̠ô' wĕyēb̠ōšû — all who were angry at him will come in shame (yēb̠ōšû, Niphal of בּוֹשׁ) — reversal of the shaming of Israel's enemies.
25: bĕYHWH yiṣdĕqû — in YHWH all the offspring of Israel will be declared righteous. The Qal *yiqtol* yiṣdĕqû — to be in right standing, to be justified — closes the chapter on the widest possible note: all the seed of Israel vindicated and glorying (yit-hallālû, Hithpa'el of הָלַל). This is the eschatological endpoint of the Cyrus oracle: political liberation serves theological vindication.
